title: RN Cardiology Emergency Study Set

flashcards.front: A client with chest pain becomes pale, diaphoretic, and hypotensive. What is the priority nursing action?
flashcards.back: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, obtain vital signs, stay with the client, and activate emergency response according to protocol.
flashcards.rationale: Unstable perfusion cues require immediate assessment and escalation before routine teaching or documentation.
flashcards.pearl: In cardiovascular deterioration, act on instability first: assess, support perfusion, and escalate.

flashcards.front: Which finding after starting nitroglycerin requires immediate follow-up?
flashcards.back: Marked hypotension, syncope, or worsening chest pain after nitroglycerin requires immediate reassessment and provider notification.
flashcards.rationale: Nitroglycerin can reduce preload and blood pressure; unsafe drops can worsen perfusion.
flashcards.pearl: Always reassess pain and blood pressure after antianginal therapy.

flashcards.front: What rhythm-related symptom is most concerning in a client with new palpitations?
flashcards.back: Syncope, chest pain, shortness of breath, hypotension, or altered mental status suggests unstable dysrhythmia.
flashcards.rationale: Symptoms of poor perfusion distinguish urgent rhythm problems from stable palpitations.
flashcards.pearl: Treat the patient, not just the monitor strip.

questions.stem: A client reports crushing chest pain and has cool clammy skin. Which action comes first?
questions.answer: Assess vital signs and activate the emergency response protocol.
questions.rationale: The client has instability cues; immediate assessment and escalation come before nonurgent interventions.

pearls: Chest pain plus diaphoresis or hypotension is unstable until proven otherwise.
Reassessment after cardiovascular medication is part of the intervention.
